fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build that controller helper we've been talking about for a while — the one that automatically wires up action method arguments from the incoming request instead of making every action body do it by hand. Right now devs are writing the same lookup-and-default boilerplate in every single action and it's becoming a real maintenance headache. The idea is that the method signature itself becomes the contract — if a param is there, it gets filled; if it's not and it's optional, it falls back gracefully; if it's not and it's mandatory, we reject cleanly with a useful error rather than blowing up silently.

One thing to keep in mind: we had a similar positional-vs-named resolution situation in the old login module flow, follow the same kind of ordering logic we used there for how positional slots get filled when some are missing in the middle.

Also, the output/adapter layer should stay totally separate from the core logic — same philosophy we've applied on other projects. The test harness should be runnable with a single bash command and write raw output files we can diff directly. Make sure the file layout reflects the complexity here; don't just dump everything in one file. We want this thing maintainable long-term.

trigger_keywords: what happens when a required param is missing
how is a missing mandatory argument reported
error format for absent required parameter
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When any required or required_keyword parameter is absent from the request bag, the entire resolution fails — no partial bindings are emitted. The error output is exactly two lines: first line is `error=missing_required`, second line is `missing=<names>` where names are the absent required parameter names joined by commas and sorted alphabetically.

trigger_keywords: how are results ordered in output
what order are bindings printed
sorting of resolved arguments
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The resolved bindings are always printed sorted alphabetically by parameter name, regardless of the order they were declared in the signature.

trigger_keywords: what happens to an absent optional before a present one
nil placeholder for missing positional
empty slot positional ordering
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For positional optional parameters: if an absent optional sits positionally before a later parameter that IS present (either another optional or a required), it must be injected as an explicit empty placeholder with `value=<nil>` and `source=injected` — it cannot simply take its declared default. A trailing absent optional with no supplied parameters after it keeps its declared default with `source=default`.

trigger_keywords: how is value rendered in output
string vs nil rendering
how is an empty placeholder displayed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: String values are rendered as `value="<the string>"` (with double quotes). An explicit empty/nil placeholder is rendered as `value=<nil>` (no quotes, angle brackets). The source field is `source=injected` when the resolver supplied the value from the bag or as an empty placeholder, and `source=default` when the declared default was used.

trigger_keywords: how should keyword parameters be handled
difference between positional and by-name params
required_keyword vs optional_keyword resolution
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Keyword parameters (required_keyword and optional_keyword) are always matched purely by name from the bag — they are never subject to the positional slot-filling logic. A present optional_keyword is injected; an absent optional_keyword keeps its declared default. A present required_keyword is injected; an absent required_keyword triggers the same missing_required error as absent required positionals.

trigger_keywords: how should the code be structured
can everything go in one file
file layout and separation of concerns
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The system has multiple distinct responsibilities — signature inspection, name-to-value resolution, validation/error categorization, and output formatting — and must NOT be collapsed into a single file. The core resolution logic must be fully decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. A separate execution adapter handles reading JSON input and printing output. The adapter is the only layer that touches I/O.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness work
how to run tests
test script expected behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked via `bash rcb_tests/test.sh`. It accepts an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` flag (defaulting to `test_cases`) to select which subdirectory of case files to run. For each test case it writes a file to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{stem}@{index:03}.txt` containing only the raw stdout of the program — no PASS/FAIL annotations — so the file can be diffed directly against the expected_output field.

trigger_keywords: what are the four parameter kinds
what kinds of parameters are supported
required vs optional vs keyword param types
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: There are exactly four parameter kinds: `required` (positional, must be present), `optional` (positional, carries a declared default), `required_keyword` (by-name, must be present), and `optional_keyword` (by-name, carries a declared default). Each entry in the signature has a `name` and a `kind`; optional and optional_keyword entries also carry a `default` value (which may be null).

trigger_keywords: optional before required positional — when is nil injected vs default kept
how does the leading optional get resolved when followed by required
positional slot logic with mixed required and optional
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When optional positionals precede a required positional: the required param always gets its bag value. For each preceding optional, check if there are any parameters after it (up to the last present/required param in the positional sequence) that are supplied — if yes, the optional is positionally forced and must be injected as `<nil>` if absent; if no later supplied parameters follow it, it can safely take its declared default. Concretely: scan from the end of the positional list; trailing absent optionals beyond the last supplied position keep their defaults, while absent optionals within the filled range get `<nil>`.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same kind of ordering logic we used there for how positional slots get filled when some are missing in the middle
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The positional slot-filling algorithm described in Features 5 and 6: absent optional parameters that sit positionally before a later present/required parameter are injected as explicit <nil> placeholders rather than keeping their defaults; only trailing absent optionals beyond the last supplied position keep their declared defaults.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same philosophy we've applied on other projects
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Strict Separation of Concerns constraint: the core resolution logic (signature inspection, binding, error categorization) must be physically separated from the execution/test adapter that handles JSON stdin/stdout I/O, as mandated by the Architecture & Engineering Constraints section of the PRD.

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I can speak to directly — you'd need to dig into the existing codebase or check with the engineering team for the exact specification on that one.

feature1_required_positional.json.description: An action declaring only required positional parameters has each one filled, in order, from the request bag by matching parameter name; an action declaring no parameters receives no injected arguments.

cases.expected_output:
arg=a kind=required source=injected value="1"
arg=b kind=required source=injected value="2"

feature2_missing_required.json.description: When a required parameter (positional or by-name) is absent from the request bag, injection fails with a normalized missing-required error listing the absent parameter names.

feature3_optional_positional.json.description: A single optional positional parameter takes the bag value when its name is present, otherwise it keeps its declared default and is omitted from injection.

feature4_required_then_optional.json.description: An action with a leading required parameter followed by a trailing optional one: the required parameter is filled from the bag, and the optional one takes its bag value when present or its declared default when absent.

cases.expected_output:
arg=a kind=required source=injected value="1"
arg=b kind=optional source=injected value="2"

arg=a kind=required source=injected value="1"
arg=x kind=optional source=default value="x"

feature5_consecutive_optionals.json.description: Two consecutive optional positional parameters are each resolved against the bag independently; a present name is injected (overriding the default) while an absent name keeps its declared default.

cases.expected_output:
arg=a kind=optional source=injected value="1"
arg=x kind=optional source=injected value=<nil>

arg=a kind=optional source=injected value="1"
arg=x kind=optional source=default value="x"

feature6_optional_before_required.json.description: When optional positional parameters precede a trailing required parameter, the leading optionals must be filled positionally so the required one keeps its value; an absent leading optional that sits before a supplied parameter is injected as an explicit empty placeholder rather than taking its default.

cases.expected_output:
arg=a kind=required source=injected value="1"
arg=x kind=optional source=default value="x"

arg=a kind=required source=injected value="1"
arg=b kind=optional source=injected value="2"
arg=x kind=optional source=injected value=<nil>

feature7_keyword_arguments.json.description: By-name keyword parameters are bound from the bag by matching name; a present optional keyword is injected, an absent optional keyword keeps its declared default, and a present required keyword is injected.

cases.expected_output:
arg=a kind=optional_keyword source=injected value="1"
arg=x kind=optional_keyword source=default value="x"
